g.data-narration: Welcome to Thread Libraries - the essential interface for creating and managing threads in modern applications.
A thread library provides the API for creating, synchronizing, and managing threads, hiding complex kernel details from programmers.
g.text: A thread library is an interface that provides
API functions for creating and managing threads.
It abstracts the underlying kernel implementation.

g.data-narration: Thread libraries can be implemented at user level (fast, but blocks entire process) or kernel level (true parallelism, but slower).
User-Level Threads run entirely in user space. The kernel sees only a single process. Fast switching but one blocking call halts everything.
g.text: Implemented entirely in user space - kernel unaware of threads.

g.data-narration: Kernel-Level Threads are managed by the OS kernel. True parallelism across CPUs is possible, but thread creation requires expensive system calls.
g.text: OS kernel manages threads directly - knows about each thread.

g.data-narration: POSIX Pthreads is the standard thread API for UNIX-like systems. It provides functions for thread creation, synchronization, and management.
g.text: Standard POSIX API (IEEE 1003.1c) for thread creation and synchronization.
Available on Linux, macOS, and most UNIX-like systems.

g.data-narration: Here's a Pthreads example that creates a thread to sum numbers from 1 to N. The main thread waits using pthread_join().
Windows Threads is a kernel-level library where every thread is known to the Windows kernel. It provides CreateThread and WaitForSingleObject.
g.text: Kernel-level thread library on Windows. Every thread is managed by the kernel.
Uses system calls directly for thread management.

g.data-narration: Windows thread example using CreateThread. The Summation function runs in the new thread, and main waits using WaitForSingleObject.
Java supports threads by extending the Thread class or implementing Runnable. This shows extending Thread - simpler but single inheritance only.
g.text: Extend the Thread class and override run(). Call start() to begin execution.

g.data-narration: Implementing Runnable is the preferred approach. Your class can still extend another class while gaining thread functionality.
g.text: Implement Runnable interface, pass to Thread. More flexible - class can still extend another.

g.data-narration: Compare Pthreads, Windows, and Java threads by platform, language, implementation type, and key functions for creation and synchronization.
Thread pools pre-create threads at startup. Requests are assigned to idle threads, avoiding expensive thread creation per request.
g.text: Pre-created threads wait for work. Faster than creating new threads per request.

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: ULT is fast but blocks entirely; KLT supports true parallelism; Pthreads is POSIX standard; Java offers flexible Runnable interface.
Exam tips: Memorize the Pthreads summation code; know extends Thread vs implements Runnable; understand ULT vs KLT trade-offs.
g.text: Write the Pthreads summation example - pthread_create, pthread_join, shared variable, runner function
extends Thread vs implements Runnable - Runnable is preferred (flexible, single inheritance)
UL vs KL threads - Key trade-off: ULT fast but blocks all on syscall; KLT slower but true parallelism
Thread pool advantages: faster, bounds resources, separates task from execution
